primary_accounting_issue: Déterminer si le revenu d’une licence de propriété intellectuelle est constaté à une date donnée ou sur la durée, selon la nature du droit concédé au client.

authority_resolution.selection_reason: I applied the strict precedence rule and selected IFRS 15 as the single governing lineage because it is the only IFRS standard in the candidate set that directly addresses revenue recognition for licences of intellectual property.
authority_resolution.residual_uncertainty: Low — the context includes specific IFRS 15 licence guidance that directly governs whether licence revenue is recognised over time or at a point in time.

primary_authority.reason: These paragraphs directly govern how to identify the licence performance obligation and whether licence revenue is recognised over time or at a point in time.

supporting_authority.reason: These paragraphs provide the general revenue recognition framework that the licence-specific guidance applies or refers back to.
These paragraphs support the over-time model by explaining how progress is measured once that model applies.
These paragraphs clarify what constitutes intellectual property and how to assess whether the licence provides access or use.
These paragraphs provide an overlay for sales-based or usage-based royalties on licences, but they do not create a separate top-level timing model.

peripheral_authority.reason: These transaction price, variable consideration, financing, non-cash consideration, and consideration payable paragraphs are not needed to identify the peer timing approaches for licence revenue.
Principal-versus-agent guidance is topically related to revenue gross or net presentation, not to the core issue of timing of licence revenue recognition.
The real-estate over-time guidance and agenda decisions are not relevant to identifying peer approaches for intellectual property licences.
These agenda decisions address tuition fees and compensation for delays or cancellations, which do not help identify the timing models for licences of intellectual property.

treatment_families.family: Distinct licence accounted for as a separate performance obligation with licence-specific timing assessment
Non-distinct licence combined with other goods or services and assessed under the general timing model

approaches.label: Right to access intellectual property
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: IFRS 15 treats a licence as over time when the nature of the promise is to provide access to intellectual property throughout the licence period.

approaches.label: Right to use intellectual property
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: IFRS 15 treats a licence as at a point in time when the nature of the promise is to provide the right to use intellectual property as it exists when granted.

approaches.label: Revenue recognised over time
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: If a licence is not distinct and is part of a broader performance obligation, IFRS 15 requires the combined obligation to be assessed under the general over-time model.

approaches.label: Revenue recognised at a point in time
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: If a licence is not distinct and the combined performance obligation does not meet the over-time criteria, IFRS 15 requires point-in-time recognition.
